q: The identification of organic compounds is based on simple chemical tests and spectroscopic techniques.
Alkanes show decolourisation when mixed with $1\%$ $KMnO_{4}$ solution.
Alkenes decolourise $1\%$ $KMnO_{4}$ solution.
Alkynes form a red coloured precipitate when passed through ammoniacal $Cu_{2}Cl_{2}$ solution.
Ethers form a yellow coloured precipitate when boiled with $HI$ and then reacted with $AgNO_{3}$.
Aldehydes form a reddish-brown coloured precipitate when heated gently with Fehling's solution.
Ketones give a reddish-brown precipitate when heated gently with Fehling's solution.
The presence of a carbonyl group such as aldehyde or ketone can be easily tested using 2,4-DNP.
If an aldehyde or ketone is present with 2,4-DNP, a deep-orange precipitate is formed.
Fehling's solution is an alkaline solution containing copper(II) ions.
Infrared (IR) spectroscopy is the study of the absorption of light by a compound in the ultraviolet region.
Electromagnetic radiation is characterised by either its frequency or its wavelength.
Wavenumber (measured in $cm^{-1}$) is the reciprocal of the wavelength.
High frequencies, large wavenumbers, and short wavelengths are associated with high energies.
Each spectroscopic technique employs a different kind of electromagnetic radiation.
IR spectroscopy detects frequencies of infrared radiation that are absorbed by a molecule to cause bond vibrations.
Stretching is a vibration that occurs between two bonds and changes the bond angle.
Only stretching and bending motions that change the dipole moment of a molecule give rise to the absorption of infrared radiation.
Homonuclear diatomic molecules like $N_{2}$ are IR active.
Heteronuclear diatomic molecules like $CO$ are IR active because they have dipole moments.
Light atoms vibrate at higher frequencies than heavier atoms.
Multiple bonds vibrate at higher frequencies than single bonds because it takes more energy to stretch a multiple bond.
The instrument used to obtain an infrared spectrum is called an IR spectrometer.
An IR spectrum is a plot of the percent transmittance or absorbance of radiation versus the wavenumber.
The higher bond polarity and more number of bonds in molecules will give a more intense absorption band.
